topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a familiar, unwelcome chime. It was from Mark. My stomach immediately tightened, a cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I hadn't spoken to him in fifteen years, not since the relentless teasing in the seventh grade had carved out a permanent space in my memory. Seeing his name pop up now, a simple request to connect, felt like a deliberate invasion. I stared at the profile picture—a forced, overly confident grin—and my jaw clenched so hard my teeth ached. Why now? What was he hoping for? I quickly minimized the message, my fingers hovering over the 'decline' button, a tremor running through my hand. It felt like a cheap trick, a way for him to try and pull me back into the awkward, painful history we shared.

[story 2]

I almost missed the ping. My focus was entirely on the intricate design of the graphic novel I was reading, but the vibration on my phone pulled my attention away. It was Mark. A friend request. A slow burn started in my chest, a hot, prickly feeling that made me want to shove the device across the room. I let the notification sit there, unacknowledged, for a full minute. When I finally looked at it, the sight of his name triggered a sharp, unpleasant tightening in my chest. I felt a sudden urge to check his recent activity, to see what life he was currently leading that somehow warranted this outreach. My breath hitched slightly, and I took a deep, shaky inhale, trying to smooth the sudden tension rising in my shoulders.

[story 3]

The screen illuminated my face, and a slow, unpleasant heat crept up my neck. Mark. Of all the people in my life, it was him. I reread the request three times, my thumb tracing the edges of the notification. A strange, bitter taste coated my mouth. It wasn't the content of the message that bothered me; it was the implication. He thought that reaching out now, after all this time, would somehow rebalance the scales. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space of my room, my movements jerky. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring, fueled by a simmering resentment that I fought hard to keep buried beneath a veneer of cool indifference.
